items.summary: Amends the list of prescribed rural housing bodies under the Title Conditions (Scotland) Act 2003 by adding Shapinsay Development Trust and Tayvallich Initiative Ltd, enabling these bodies to benefit from rural housing burdens including rights of pre-emption on properties.
items.reason: This order perpetuates and expands government intervention by favouring specific trusts with special pre-emption rights over private property, restricting owners' ability to sell freely to the highest bidder. Such distortions reduce property market liquidity, discourage investment in rural areas, create uncertainty for owners, and pick winners among non-profits without market validation. Unseen costs include stifled rural housing supply and development, as properties are locked into subsidized uses, harming prosperity and individualism; markets, not government lists, should determine housing solutions.

items.title: The Property Factors (Registration) (Scotland) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Regulations mandating additional information on controllers, basic criminal conviction checks, and tiered registration fees (£200 for ≤100 properties, £750 for >100, indexed to CPI) for property factors under the Property Factors (Scotland) Act 2011.
items.reason: These create barriers to entry via compliance costs and fees, distorting incentives, deterring small operators, reducing competition in property management, raising service prices, and harming consumers through less choice and innovation; market reputation and contracts suffice for quality assurance without state licensing, which protects incumbents more than residents.

items.title: The Registration of Births, Still-births, Deaths and Marriages (Prescription of Forms) (Scotland) Amendment Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amends the 1997 Regulations prescribing forms for applications to record or change forenames/surnames of children under 12 months, under 16, and persons over 16 in Scotland's birth/death/marriage registry, effective 1 November 2024.
items.reason: Civil registration of vital events and name changes supports verifiable identity essential for contracts, property rights, and markets. Updating forms ensures administrative efficiency without new burdens; deletion would revert to outdated forms, causing processing delays, errors, and higher unseen costs for individuals exercising personal liberty in name changes, without which the institution fails to function smoothly.

items.title: The A99 Trunk Road (Wick) (Temporary Prohibition on Waiting, Loading and Unloading) Order 2024
items.summary: Temporary order prohibiting waiting, loading, and unloading on a specific section of the A99 Trunk Road in Wick, Scotland, likely for roadworks or safety.
items.reason: This hyper-local, temporary regulation imposes restrictions on individual liberty and commercial activity (e.g., delivery vans, residents parking), with unseen costs including administrative overhead for drafting/enforcing such orders, diverting public resources from productive uses; proliferates regulatory bloat across thousands of similar instruments, distorting incentives for efficient road use and setting precedent for excessive government micromanagement of public spaces where private coordination or simple signage could suffice without statutory force.

items.title: The A85 Trunk Road (Oban) (Temporary Prohibition on Use of Road) (No. 4) Order 2024
items.summary: Scottish Statutory Instrument temporarily prohibiting use of a specific section of the A85 Trunk Road in Oban, likely for safety, maintenance, or emergency purposes (No. 4 Order 2024).
items.reason: Deletion would prevent necessary temporary road closures, leaving British citizens worse off by exposing them to hazards like unsafe repairs or accidents on deteriorating infrastructure; without this authority, public road safety—a basic function for commerce and mobility—could not be reliably maintained, as private incentives alone would underprovide such interventions on public goods.

items.title: The M90/A90 (Finavon Bridge) (Temporary Vehicle Weight Restriction) Order 2024
items.summary: Temporary Scottish Statutory Instrument imposing weight restrictions on vehicles using the Finavon Bridge on the M90/A90 road to protect its structural integrity.
items.reason: Deleting this would expose the public to immediate risks of bridge damage or collapse from overweight vehicles, causing loss of life, injuries, and massive taxpayer-funded repairs; public infrastructure lacks private property incentives for self-enforcement, so temporary government restrictions prevent greater harms and costs without distorting broader markets.

items.title: The A82 Trunk Road (North Ballachulish) (Temporary Prohibition on Use of Road) Order 2024
items.summary: Temporary Scottish Statutory Instrument prohibiting use of a specific section of the A82 Trunk Road at North Ballachulish, likely for road maintenance, safety works, or emergencies.
items.reason: This limited-duration order provides legal authority to enforce a road closure, enabling safe execution of necessary public road maintenance or repairs. Without it, authorities lack clear statutory power to prohibit access, risking unsafe conditions during works—endangering drivers, pedestrians, and workers with potential accidents, injuries, or fatalities. British citizens would be worse off with heightened road safety risks and disrupted long-term infrastructure reliability, outcomes not reliably achieved via informal measures or common law alone, as formal prohibition ensures compliance and liability protection. Costs are minimal (temporary, targeted) versus clear safety benefits outweighing distortions like brief detours.

items.title: The A85 Trunk Road (Comrie) (Temporary Prohibition on Waiting, Loading and Unloading) Order 2024
items.summary: Temporary Scottish Statutory Instrument prohibiting waiting, loading, and unloading on a specific section of the A85 Trunk Road in Comrie.
items.reason: This hyper-local, temporary traffic restriction exemplifies regulatory overreach, curtailing individual liberty to use public roads (funded by taxpayers) for presumed safety or works, with unseen costs including distorted travel incentives, local business disruptions, enforcement bureaucracy, and reduced economic activity from delays. Such orders proliferate under enabling acts, accumulating to hinder spontaneous order and prosperity; voluntary compliance, signage, or private coordination would emerge without state coercion.

items.title: The A85 Trunk Road (Comrie) (Temporary Prohibition on Waiting, Loading and Unloading) (No. 6) Order 2024
items.summary: Temporary Scottish Statutory Instrument prohibiting waiting, loading, and unloading on a specific section of the A85 Trunk Road in Comrie (No. 6 Order 2024).
items.reason: This hyper-local, temporary traffic restriction exemplifies regulatory overreach, imposing compliance costs on drivers and businesses, distorting local traffic incentives, increasing travel times, and suppressing economic activity in Comrie with unseen consequences like reduced commerce and delivery efficiency. Such ad-hoc bureaucratic interventions accumulate to burden liberty and prosperity; private coordination, signage, or common-law liability would suffice without government decree, avoiding precedent for endless similar orders.

items.title: The Registration of Births, Deaths and Marriages (Scotland) Act 1965 (Prohibition on Disposal of a Body without Authorisation) Amendment Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amendment to Scottish regulations on body disposal, requiring coroner’s authorisation for burial or cremation in Scotland when the death occurred in England, Wales or Northern Ireland and has not been registered.
items.reason: This narrow administrative rule ensures cross-UK jurisdictional coordination for death verification, preventing unauthorized body disposals that could enable fraud, hide crimes, spread health risks, or cause legal disputes. Deleting it would leave a gap where private actors (e.g., funeral directors) might proceed without checks, leading to unseen harms like delayed investigations or public safety issues; families and society would be worse off without this minimal institutional safeguard, which does not distort markets or incentives broadly but upholds basic order relied upon by individuals.

items.title: The A85 Trunk Road (Crieff) (Temporary Prohibition on Waiting, Loading and Unloading) Order 2024

items.title: The Good Food Nation (Scotland) Act 2022 (Commencement No. 2) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Commencement regulations bringing into force sections of the Good Food Nation (Scotland) Act 2022, including provisions for establishing the Scottish Food Commission (effective 15 February 2024) and other related measures (effective 30 June 2025).
items.reason: Establishes a new bureaucratic Scottish Food Commission and enables related interventions in food policy, distorting market incentives for producers and suppliers, raising costs through taxpayer-funded planning and oversight, stifling innovation and private enterprise in Scotland's food sector, with unseen consequences like reduced supply, higher prices for consumers (especially the poor), and regulatory capture by special interests—outcomes that free markets achieve better without such institutions.

items.title: The M90 Trunk Road Friarton Bridge (Temporary Vehicle Weight Restriction) Order 2024
items.summary: Temporary Scottish Statutory Instrument imposing vehicle weight restrictions on the Friarton Bridge of the M90 trunk road, likely for safety or maintenance reasons.
items.reason: This hyper-specific, temporary regulation exemplifies bureaucratic overreach on public infrastructure, imposing unseen costs such as traffic diversions, delays, higher fuel/logistics expenses for hauliers, and distorted transport incentives that raise goods prices and hinder economic efficiency. Without private property rights in roads, such interventions perpetuate government monopoly distortions; market institutions (e.g., private toll roads) would handle weight limits dynamically via signs, fees, or closures without statutory bloat, avoiding compliance burdens and promoting liberty/prosperity.

items.title: The A737 Trunk Road (Kilwinning) (Temporary Prohibition on Waiting, Loading and Unloading) Order 2024
items.summary: Temporary order prohibiting waiting, loading, and unloading on a specific section of the A737 Trunk Road in Kilwinning, Scotland, likely for roadworks or traffic management.
items.reason: Deleting this would leave citizens worse off by permitting unsafe parking/loading on a public road during temporary disruptions, increasing accident risks, traffic congestion, and delays without any institutional alternative for quick, targeted safety enforcement; general road rules lack the specificity needed here, and private incentives wouldn't spontaneously resolve acute public road hazards.

items.title: The Town and Country Planning (Fees for Applications) (Scotland) Amendment Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amends the Town and Country Planning (Fees for Applications) (Scotland) Regulations 2022 to increase fees for various planning applications, advertisements, hazardous substances, and approvals; introduces new fees for processing agreements and masterplan consent area schemes; and adjusts fee schedules.
items.reason: These fee increases and new charges act as a tax on development, raising barriers to entry for housing and commercial projects, reducing supply, inflating costs, and exacerbating shortages that harm affordability and economic growth, especially for lower-income citizens. Unseen consequences include deterring small developers and entrepreneurs, entrenching monopolies for large firms able to absorb costs, distorting land markets, and funding an inefficient bureaucratic monopoly on property rights without improving outcomes.

items.title: The Diligence against Earnings (Variation) (Scotland) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Scottish Statutory Instrument updating figures and deduction tables in the Debtors (Scotland) Act 1987 for earnings arrestments and maintenance arrestments, increasing a deduction sum from £21.56 to £24.66 and substituting new Tables A, B, and C, primarily for inflation adjustment.
items.reason: Perpetuates bureaucratic intervention in private debt enforcement by requiring periodic government updates to deduction amounts and tables, imposing compliance costs on employers and creating regulatory churn. Unseen consequences include distorted incentives for creditors (reduced real recovery if updates lag) and debtors (rigid rules over simple percentages), hindering flexible market-based credit and employment decisions; better achieved via fixed, minimal statutory rules or court discretion without annual fiddling.

items.title: The Plant Health (Import Inspection Fees) (Scotland) Amendment (No. 2) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amends the Plant Health (Import Inspection Fees) (Scotland) Regulations 2014 by changing the dates for payment of fees charged for documentary, physical, and identity checks on plant consignments imported into Scotland from EU Member States, Liechtenstein, or Switzerland.
items.reason: This amendment perpetuates a system of government-imposed fees for mandatory inspections, functioning as a tax on imports that distorts trade incentives, increases costs for importers and ultimately consumers, and creates administrative burdens. Unseen consequences include reduced plant supply, higher prices, barriers to entry for smaller businesses, and protectionist effects favoring domestic producers at the expense of competition and prosperity; private markets, reputation, and voluntary certification would handle plant health risks more efficiently without state coercion.

items.title: The Firefighters’ Pension Schemes (Scotland) Amendment (No. 2) Order 2024
items.summary: Amends the Firefighters’ Pension Scheme (Scotland) Order 2007 to extend the period for retained firefighters in Scotland to access pensions and death grants, aligning rules with the rest of the UK.
items.reason: Public sector pensions like this create unfunded liabilities borne by taxpayers, distort labor markets by offering superior benefits to government employees versus private sector workers, and lock in dependency on state provision rather than private savings and property rights. This minor alignment perpetuates the institution's unseen costs—higher taxes, debt accumulation, reduced economic efficiency—without evidence that market incentives couldn't better attract and retain firefighters through competitive pay and private pensions.

items.title: The A84 Trunk Road (Doune) (Temporary Prohibition on Use of Road) Order 2024

items.reason: This hyper-specific, temporary road closure order exemplifies bureaucratic overreach, requiring formal statutory processes for routine infrastructure management. Unseen costs include administrative expenses to draft and enact such instruments, delays in roadworks due to red tape, economic losses from mandated detours increasing transport costs and time for drivers, and distorted incentives for efficient road maintenance as government monopoly control supplants market or local solutions. Private road owners or decentralized authorities could close segments contractually or pragmatically without central fiat, fostering innovation and responsiveness while avoiding precedent for endless micro-regulations.

Institutions require such minimal powers for public goods like roads, where private alternatives aren't immediately viable, outweighing short-term inconvenience.

items.title: The Non-Domestic Rate (Scotland) Order 2024

items.reason: This Order imposes a mandatory property tax on businesses, distorting incentives for investment, expansion, and location choices, which raises costs, reduces supply of goods/services, encourages relocations to lower-tax jurisdictions, and stifles entrepreneurship. Unseen consequences include foregone wealth creation, job losses, and inefficient resource allocation, as government revenue extraction via such taxes consistently fails to deliver proportional benefits while eroding private property rights and economic liberty.

items.reason: Deleting this amendment would retain Tain Police Station on the list despite it being unsuitable (likely closed), causing confusion for offenders, potential non-compliance with notification duties, increased administrative burden on police, and heightened public safety risks from unmonitored offenders; the regulation ensures efficient enforcement of existing requirements without distorting incentives or imposing undue costs.

items.summary: Scottish Statutory Instrument temporarily prohibiting use of a specific section of the A96 Trunk Road in Nairn for a limited period, likely for maintenance or safety reasons.

items.title: The Dangerous Dogs (Designated Types) (Scotland) Order 2024
items.summary: This Scottish Statutory Instrument designates the XL Bully dog breed under section 1 of the Dangerous Dogs Act 1991, appointing 31 July 2024 as the date after which possession becomes unlawful (with exemptions), effectively banning the breed.
items.reason: Breed-specific bans infringe on private property rights and individual liberty by preemptively criminalizing ownership based on type rather than behavior, ignoring responsible owners and proven dogs. Unintended consequences include black markets fostering irresponsible breeding/hiding of dogs, enforcement costs diverting police resources, and distorted incentives in the pet industry without evidence that such bans reduce attacks more effectively than behavior-based liability laws. Institutions favoring liberty (e.g., strict owner accountability) better protect public safety without government overreach.

items.title: The Council Tax Reduction (Scotland) Amendment (No. 3) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amendments to Scottish Council Tax Reduction Regulations (2012 and 2021) disregarding compensation payments from the infected blood payment scheme (and related estate payments) from income and capital calculations for eligibility, including for non-dependants and second adults.
items.reason: This adds further complexity to an already convoluted welfare means-testing system, creating arbitrary disregards for specific payments that distort incentives and equity (why these but not other compensations?). Administrative burdens on councils rise, with unseen costs in compliance, errors, and precedent for endless special pleading. Perpetuates welfare dependency rather than letting recipients fully own and spend private compensation property freely, undermining liberty and individualism. Root issues (scandal compensation) should be addressed directly, not via benefit patches that bloat regulations without creating wealth.

items.title: The Disability Assistance (Scottish Adult Disability Living Allowance) (Consequential Amendment, Revocation and Saving Provision) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Consequential and transitional amendments to secondary legislation to introduce Scottish Adult Disability Living Allowance, replacing Disability Living Allowance for a closed cohort of adults in Scotland not transferred to Personal Independence Payment, ensuring equivalent entitlements and revoking prior transitional rules.
items.reason: This perpetuates the welfare state's transfer mechanisms, funded by distortive taxation that reduces private investment and productivity; administrative overhead diverts resources from wealth creation, creates dependency disincentives for work or private insurance, and imposes unseen compliance burdens on citizens and firms via legal complexity; without it, market-driven solutions like private disability insurance or charity would emerge, fostering self-reliance and innovation over government paternalism.

items.title: The A83 Trunk Road (Cairndow) (Temporary Prohibition on Use of Road) Order 2024
items.summary: Temporary prohibition order on a specific section of the A83 Trunk Road at Cairndow, Scotland, likely for safety, maintenance, or emergency reasons.
items.reason: Without this legislation, drivers would face heightened risks of accidents, injuries, or fatalities if the road section was hazardous (e.g., due to repairs or weather damage), imposing direct costs on individuals and the economy via emergency services, insurance, and lost productivity. Public road stewardship requires such minimal, targeted interventions to protect life and property, mirroring what private owners would do; deletion would leave no mechanism for immediate enforcement, worsening outcomes for users who cannot assess hidden dangers.

items.title: The Protection of Vulnerable Groups (Referrals by Chief Constable) (Prescribed Information) (Scotland) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Scottish regulations made under the Protection of Vulnerable Groups (Scotland) Act 2007, prescribing the information that the chief constable must provide to Scottish Ministers about individuals performing regulated roles (e.g., with vulnerable groups) without participating in the PVG vetting scheme.
items.reason: This regulation enforces bureaucratic compliance in the PVG scheme by mandating police reporting, adding administrative costs to law enforcement and government without clear evidence of superior protection compared to market alternatives like private vetting, employer liability insurance, reputation mechanisms, and contractual safeguards. Unintended consequences include labor market distortions in care/education sectors—deterring workers via delays, fees, and privacy intrusions—leading to shortages that harm vulnerable groups more than isolated risks, while inflating costs passed to families and taxpayers; institutions like private property and tort law better align incentives for safety without central mandates.

items.title: The Protection of Vulnerable Groups (Information for Listing and Vetting) (Scotland) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Regulations prescribing specific details of convictions, cautions, civil orders, and other relevant matters from police records to be provided to Scottish Ministers for listing decisions and vetting scheme members under the Protection of Vulnerable Groups (Scotland) Act 2007.
items.reason: This administrative prescription expands government-mandated information sharing and vetting, imposing unseen costs like heightened barriers to entry in caring professions (fewer workers, higher service costs, reduced supply/choice for families), privacy erosion, bureaucratic overhead on police/ministers, and distortion of labor incentives; private markets could innovate better via reputation, insurance, contracts, and voluntary disclosures without central monopoly.

items.title: The Level 1 and Level 2 Disclosure Information (Scotland) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Regulations under Disclosure (Scotland) Act 2020 detailing content of Level 1 and Level 2 criminal record disclosures, including unspent convictions, criminal disposals, barring information, civil court orders for roles with children/adults, central databases, and fingerprinting for identity verification.
items.reason: Mandates bureaucratic standardization of criminal disclosures, imposing compliance costs, privacy intrusions, and labor market distortions by barring reformed offenders from vulnerable-sector jobs; unseen consequences include reduced employment incentives, stifled private vetting innovation, and Hayekian knowledge problem where government presumes to define 'relevant' info better than employers.

items.title: The Consideration of Suitability for Regulated Roles (Prescribed Purposes) (Scotland) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Scottish Statutory Instrument prescribing additional purposes under Section 68 of the Disclosure (Scotland) Act 2020, expanding references in sections 17(1)(b) and 44(2) to include more scenarios for considering an individual's suitability for regulated roles (e.g., health care, social work) under the Protecting Vulnerable Groups (PVG) Scheme.
items.reason: This regulation expands mandatory government vetting requirements via the bureaucratic PVG Scheme, imposing compliance costs, delays, and barriers to entry for workers and volunteers in care sectors. Unintended consequences include labor shortages harming vulnerable groups, reduced supply of services, higher costs distorting incentives, and erosion of liberty through state monopoly on disclosures; private markets could provide faster, more accurate checks without these distortions.

items.title: The Disclosure and Use of Level 2 Disclosures (Prescribed Purpose and Circumstances) (Scotland) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Prescribes specific exceptions to offences in the Disclosure (Scotland) Act 2020 prohibiting unlawful disclosure or seeking of Level 2 criminal record disclosures, allowing such actions when employers provide transport for children or protected adults to schools, hospitals, etc., under arrangements with councils, schools, or health bodies.
items.reason: Deleting this would leave transport providers for vulnerable children and protected adults unable to access necessary Level 2 disclosures for vetting drivers and staff, increasing risks of harm in safety-critical roles reliant on public contracts; the narrow exceptions enable targeted safety checks without broad liberty infringements or significant unintended costs like bureaucracy or market distortion.

items.summary: Temporary Scottish Statutory Instrument prohibiting use of a specific section of the A83 Trunk Road in Ardrishaig for safety or maintenance purposes.

items.title: The A702 Trunk Road (Hillend) (Prohibition on Waiting, Loading and Unloading) Order 2024
items.summary: Scottish Statutory Instrument prohibiting waiting, loading, and unloading on a specific section of the A702 trunk road at Hillend.
items.reason: This hyper-local traffic regulation maintains safety and flow on a major public trunk road, preventing hazardous parking or stopping that could cause accidents, congestion, or block emergency access. items.title: The A83 Trunk Road (Tarbert) (Temporary Prohibition on Use of Road) (No. 2) Order 2
items.summary: Scottish Statutory Instrument 2024 No. 321 imposing a temporary prohibition on use of a specific section of the A83 Trunk Road in Tarbert, likely for maintenance, safety, or emergency reasons.

items.title: The Coronavirus (Recovery and Reform) (Scotland) Act 2022 (Extension of Temporary Justice Measures) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Extends the expiry of temporary justice measures under the Coronavirus (Recovery and Reform) (Scotland) Act 2022 from 30 November 2024 to 30 November 2025.
items.reason: These COVID-era temporary measures have been repeatedly extended, risking their entrenchment as permanent fixtures despite the pandemic's end; this perpetuates distorted incentives in the justice system, expands state powers with potential for abuse (e.g., surveillance, reduced due process), inflates bureaucratic costs, and hinders efficient private incentives for legal services and property rights enforcement, all without evidence of net benefits outweighing liberty erosions.

items.title: Act of Sederunt (Rules of the Court of Session 1994, Sheriff Appeal Court Rules 2021 and Ordinary Cause Rules 1993 Amendment) (Taxation of Judicial Expenses) 2024
items.summary: Act of Sederunt amending Scottish court rules (Court of Session 1994, Sheriff Appeal Court 2021, Ordinary Cause 1993) to extend the timeframe for lodging objections prior to a diet of taxation from 3 business days to 14 calendar days.
items.reason: Procedural rules enabling fairer resolution of legal costs are essential for upholding property rights and contract enforcement, core to liberty and prosperity. Deleting this reverts to a 3-business-day window, heightening rushed errors in taxation, unfair billings, and disputes, raising litigation costs and distorting incentives for efficient dispute resolution without ensuring better outcomes.

items.title: The Official Controls (Import of High Risk Food and Feed of Non-Animal Origin) Amendment (Scotland) (No. 2) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amendments to retained EU Regulation 2019/1793 imposing temporary increased official controls, sampling, and special entry conditions on specific high-risk food and feed imports (e.g., guar gum, items prone to mycotoxins, aflatoxins, pesticides, microbiological contamination) from certain third countries into Great Britain.
items.reason: These regulations create trade barriers through mandatory border checks, sampling, and restrictions, distorting incentives for importers, raising costs passed to consumers, reducing supply of affordable goods, and potentially protecting domestic producers. Unintended consequences include higher food prices harming the poor, supply shortages, bureaucratic delays, and stifled competition; private markets, via reputation, contracts, liability, and voluntary certification, better ensure safety without government intervention, as historical evidence shows regulations often fail to deliver net benefits and crowd out superior private institutions.

items.title: The Dumfries and Galloway Council (Stranraer) Harbour Revision Order 2024
items.summary: Designates Dumfries and Galloway Council as harbour authority for Stranraer Harbour, defines limits, grants powers for leasing, byelaws, directions, and repeals obsolete provisions.
items.reason: Deletion would leave no designated authority for a key regional port vital for trade and ferries, risking safety hazards, navigation chaos (tragedy of the commons), maintenance neglect, and economic disruption harming local prosperity and jobs. Council's local accountability ensures coordinated management and safety standards that markets alone might not spontaneously provide without initial institutional framework; repeals reduce legal bloat without distorting broader incentives.

items.title: The Food Safety (Sampling and Qualifications) (Scotland) Amendment Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amends the Food Safety (Sampling and Qualifications) (Scotland) Regulations 2013 to recognize professional qualifications and experience of food examiners from Switzerland (and specified states) under the UK-Switzerland free trade agreement on professional qualifications.
items.reason: This regulation promotes free trade and labor mobility by reducing artificial barriers to entry for qualified foreign professionals, increasing the supply of food examiners, enhancing competition, and lowering costs for food safety enforcement without imposing new restrictions. Deleting it would breach a trade agreement, limit expertise available in Scotland, raise compliance costs for businesses through supply shortages, and harm UK's prosperity by signaling unreliability in international commitments—outcomes not achievable through market processes alone given existing qualification mandates.

items.title: The A977/A876 Trunk Road (Kincardine) (Temporary 20mph Speed Restriction) Order 2024
items.summary: Temporary order imposing a 20mph speed restriction on the A977/A876 Trunk Road in Kincardine, Scotland.
items.reason: This hyper-local, temporary regulation exemplifies bureaucratic overreach, imposing restrictions on individual liberty and road users' time preferences. It incurs unseen costs including administrative creation/enforcement expenses, traffic delays, increased idling emissions/fuel use, and distorted incentives for safe driving (e.g., bunching speeds rather than natural adjustment). Absent evidence of net safety gains outweighing these—speed limits often fail to reduce accidents overall per economic studies—deletion allows voluntary, context-aware driver behavior, fostering prosperity via reduced regulatory drag.

items.title: The A9 and A95 Trunk Roads (Dalraddy to Slochd) (Trunking) Order 2024
items.summary: Designates specific sections of the A9 and A95 roads from Dalraddy to Slochd in Scotland as trunk roads, transferring maintenance and management responsibility to the national trunk road authority (Transport Scotland).
items.reason: This administrative order facilitates the upgrading and consistent management of critical transport infrastructure vital for commerce, trade, and mobility in the Scottish Highlands. Deleting it would leave these key routes under under-resourced local authority control, leading to poorer maintenance, safety risks, delays in dualling projects, reduced economic connectivity, and unseen costs like hindered business travel, tourism, and freight efficiency—outweighing minimal regulatory burden as it enables wealth creation through better infrastructure without distorting private incentives.

items.title: The A9 and A95 Trunk Roads (Dalraddy to Slochd) (Side Roads) Order 2024
items.summary: Scottish Statutory Instrument authorizing the stopping up, diversion, and provision of new side roads to facilitate improvements (likely dualling) on the A9 and A95 trunk roads between Dalraddy and Slochd.
items.reason: This order exercises state compulsion over property rights (private land access, existing roads) to enable a government-directed infrastructure project, perpetuating a government monopoly on roads. Keeping it distorts incentives by diverting taxpayer funds from private productive uses to politically prioritized projects, ignores market pricing of road demand (e.g., tolls), creates unseen costs like misallocated resources, environmental externalities not internalized, and dependency on bureaucratic planning prone to capture and inefficiency. Without coercive orders, private enterprise could innovate better transport solutions via voluntary contracts, fostering liberty and true prosperity.

items.title: The Rural Development (Continuation of Operation) (Miscellaneous Amendment) (Scotland) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amendments to extend operational end dates and set payment rates for assimilated EU rural development laws in Scotland, including the Less Favoured Area Support Scheme, providing subsidies to farmers in less favoured areas.
items.reason: These subsidies distort agricultural incentives by propping up operations in marginal areas that markets would otherwise reallocate to more productive uses, fostering dependency on government handouts rather than innovation and efficiency; unseen costs include taxpayer burdens, reduced overall productivity, and perpetuation of rural poverty through misallocated resources instead of genuine market-driven prosperity.

items.title: The A9 Trunk Road (Helmsdale) (Temporary 20mph Speed Restriction) Order 2024
items.summary: Temporary Scottish Statutory Instrument imposing a 20mph speed restriction on a specific section of the A9 trunk road at Helmsdale, likely for roadworks or safety.
items.reason: This hyper-local, temporary regulation exemplifies government overreach, imposing uniform speed limits that distort individual incentives to travel efficiently and safely based on personal judgment. Unseen costs include increased travel times, higher fuel consumption, congestion, and economic losses for drivers and businesses, while bureaucratic overhead to enact such micro-orders diverts resources. Safety in hazard zones can be managed via engineering solutions, signage, or voluntary compliance without coercive legislation, aligning with liberty and private risk assessment over nanny-state intervention.

items.title: The A9 Trunk Road (Golspie) (Temporary 20mph Speed Restriction) Order 2024
items.summary: Scottish Statutory Instrument imposing a temporary 20mph speed restriction on a section of the A9 trunk road in Golspie.
items.reason: This hyper-local, temporary regulation exemplifies bureaucratic overreach, imposing unseen costs such as increased travel times, higher fuel consumption from stop-start driving, delays for commerce and emergency services, and distorted incentives for road users. Private property and liberty principles suggest individuals, via personal responsibility, insurance markets, and voluntary caution, would navigate risks more efficiently without such mandates. The cumulative administrative burden of myriad similar orders erodes prosperity without clear evidence of net safety gains outweighing liberty losses.

items.title: The A83 Trunk Road (Campbeltown) (Temporary Prohibition on Use of Road) (No. 2) Order 2024
items.summary: Scottish Statutory Instrument temporarily prohibiting use of a specific section of the A83 Trunk Road near Campbeltown, likely for safety or maintenance reasons.
items.reason: Deleting this would expose road users to hazards (e.g., roadworks, landslides) that prompted the closure, increasing risks of accidents, injuries, and fatalities; without this targeted order, authorities could not enforce the prohibition, failing to deliver the safety outcome that private warnings or alternatives cannot reliably achieve on public infrastructure.

items.title: The M9/A9 Trunk Road (Thurso and Scrabster) (Temporary 20mph, 30mph and 40mph Speed Restrictions) Order 2024
items.summary: Scottish Statutory Instrument 2024 No. 346 imposing temporary 20mph, 30mph, and 40mph speed restrictions on sections of the M9/A9 trunk road near Thurso and Scrabster, likely for roadworks or maintenance.
items.reason: This temporary order exemplifies bureaucratic overreach on government-owned roads, restricting individual liberty and judgment of speed based on conditions. Drivers are capable of self-regulating risks, especially temporarily; enforcement diverts police resources, causes delays increasing fuel costs and time lost (unseen economic drag on local businesses and commuters), and sets precedent for endless regulatory tweaks. Privatized roads would handle via tolls/insurance incentives without state mandates. Costs outweigh safety claims, as regulations distort incentives without proven net benefits.

items.title: Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act 2024
items.summary: The Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act 2024 bans new long residential leasehold houses (with limited exceptions), reforms enfranchisement and lease extension rights (removing qualifying periods, extending to 990 years at peppercorn rent, new 'market value' valuation without marriage/hope value), regulates service charges/estate management/rentcharges with new transparency/demand rules, mandates redress schemes, and amends building safety remediation.
items.reason: This Act massively interferes with property rights and voluntary contracts by banning a legitimate housing tenure (leasehold houses), forcing freehold-only development which distorts supply, raises costs (developers avoid houses or inflate prices), and reduces options; cheapens enfranchisement/extensions via forced wealth transfer from freeholders to leaseholders (new valuation ignores hope/marriage value, peppercorn rents), eroding incentives to invest in/build/manage properties; piles on regulations (service charge demands/reports, insurance limits, tribunals) creating compliance burdens, unseen deadweight losses (exit of landlords, less maintenance/supply, bureaucracy explosion); institutions of leasehold enabled prosperity via secure long-term ownership without full capital outlay, but this decree overrides market, suspecting politicians' 'fairness' rhetoric hides anti-property bias hurting poor via scarcity/higher rents.

items.title: Supply and Appropriation (Main Estimates) Act 2024
items.summary: Annual parliamentary authorization for government use of resources up to £566.9 billion and issuance from the Consolidated Fund up to £464 billion for the fiscal year ending 31 March 2025, appropriating funds across departments including Health (£173bn net cash), Education (£111bn), Defence (£56bn), and others totaling over £1 trillion in gross expenditure, enabling all public spending.
items.reason: This act perpetuates a colossal state apparatus with over £1 trillion in spending, funding welfare redistribution, state monopolies in health/education, bloated regulation, and deficit-financed consumption rather than productive investment. Keeping it imposes massive deadweight losses from taxation (distorting incentives, reducing supply), moral hazard (welfare dependency, reduced work effort), crowding out (private savings/investment diverted to low-ROI government uses), inflation/debt accumulation (future tax burdens), and institutional entrenchment of political rent-seeking over market-driven prosperity. Deletion would halt unauthorized spending, forcing fiscal discipline, lower taxes, and resource reallocation to private property/enterprise, yielding unseen gains in growth, individualism, and liberty as per Mises/Hayek/Friedman critiques of interventionism.

items.title: Budget Responsibility Act 2024
items.summary: The Budget Responsibility Act 2024 amends the 2011 Act to require the Treasury to commission independent Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) reports on fiscally significant measures before parliamentary announcements, with OBR able to act independently if not requested; exceptions for emergencies.
items.reason: This legislation institutionalizes independent fiscal scrutiny, constraining discretionary government spending announcements that could lead to unaffordable deficits, debt accumulation, inflation, and tax hikes without transparency. Deleting it would allow politicians to make fiscally reckless commitments unchecked, distorting incentives for sound budgeting and eroding private property through hidden fiscal burdens; British citizens would be worse off with higher long-term costs to liberty and prosperity, as historical evidence shows unconstrained fiscal policy fails to deliver promised outcomes while creating unseen debt overhangs.

items.title: Passenger Railway Services (Public Ownership) Act 2024
items.summary: Prohibits extensions of existing private franchises and new private franchises for passenger railway services, mandating future provision by public sector companies wholly or jointly owned by UK government ministers, effectively nationalizing passenger rail services.
items.reason: Mandates state ownership of passenger rail, eliminating private incentives, competition, and profit motives that drove post-privatization improvements in ridership, investment, and service quality. Creates bureaucratic monopoly prone to inefficiency, ballooning subsidies (already £12bn+ annually), and distorted incentives where losses are socialized but gains absent; unseen costs include deterred private investment, higher taxes/debt crowding out productive uses, reduced innovation, and failure to address root issues like planning restrictions, harming prosperity and liberty.

items.title: Finance Act 2024
items.summary: The Finance Act 2024 makes extensive changes to UK tax rules, including permanent full expensing for plant/machinery, a new R&D tax credit regime for companies (replacing prior schemes with enhanced SME reliefs), audiovisual/video game tax credits (replacing prior film/TV/video game reliefs), amendments to theatre/orchestra/museum reliefs, abolition of lifetime allowance charge with new lump sum allowances, default cash basis for small trades (removing turnover limits/restrictions), increases in various excise duties (tobacco/vehicles/air passengers/landfill/aggregates/plastic tax), anti-avoidance measures, and miscellaneous provisions (e.g., REIT/tax tonnage tweaks, Pillar Two alignment).
items.reason: This Act largely enhances incentives for investment, innovation, saving, and simplifies compliance, aligning with institutions that foster wealth creation via private property and undistorted markets. Permanent full expensing and R&D reforms reduce effective tax rates on capital/R&D, countering regulatory distortions and boosting supply/productivity without decree-based redistribution. Pension changes remove LTA barrier, promoting individual liberty in retirement planning. Cash basis default cuts bureaucracy for small traders (unseen compliance savings outweigh minor loss offsets). Creative reliefs, while sector-specific, expand UK cultural output vs. status quo. Excise hikes on vices/polluters have unseen costs (black markets, evasion) but minor relative to pro-growth reforms; anti-avoidance expands state power (costly enforcement). Without it, UK reverts to temporary expensing, stingier R&D (hurting SMEs), LTA distortions (reduced saving), complex accounting (stifling entrepreneurship), forgoing prosperity gains. Net, institutions strengthened for liberty/wealth; unseen benefits (investment surge) exceed costs.

items.title: Supply and Appropriation (Anticipation and Adjustments) Act 2024
items.summary: An Act authorising government resource use and Consolidated Fund issues for fiscal years ending 31 March 2023, 2024, and 2025, including a Vote on Account of £472bn for 2024-25, supplementary provisions increasing 2023-24 spending by £18.6bn net, and excesses of £0.95bn for 2022-23 across departments like Health, Education, Defence, with detailed schedules of departmental estimates.
items.reason: This Act enables vast government spending (£472bn Vote on Account alone) across inefficient departments, funded by taxation, borrowing, and money creation, distorting price signals, crowding out private investment, inflating costs in health/education/welfare, fostering dependency, and creating moral hazard where politicians promise aid without institutional safeguards against waste, capital flight, or rich evasion. Unseen costs include reduced innovation, entrepreneurship stifled by high taxes/debt, intergenerational burdens from £2.7tn+ national debt interest, and failure to lift poor via liberty/property rights, as rhetoric ignores incentive perversion and supply shortages from state monopoly.

items.title: National Insurance Contributions (Reduction in Rates) Act 2024
items.summary: Act reducing main rates of primary Class 1 National Insurance contributions from 10% to 8% (and reduced rate from 3.85% to 1.85%) and Class 4 contributions from 9% to 6%, with adjustments to annual maxima, effective from 6 April 2024.
items.reason: Deleting this Act would prevent these tax reductions, maintaining higher National Insurance rates that distort labor incentives, reduce workers' and self-employed individuals' take-home pay, discourage employment and entrepreneurship, and slow wealth creation through reduced disposable income and economic activity—outcomes that would persist without this liberty-enhancing reform, as government does not spontaneously lower taxes.

items.title: Trade (Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership) Act 2024
items.summary: Enables UK implementation of the Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership (CPTPP) by amending legislation on conformity assessment bodies, public procurement rules to include CPTPP partners, designations of origin/geographical indications, and performers' rights to align with agreement obligations.
items.reason: CPTPP is a multilateral free trade agreement reducing tariffs and barriers across 11 economies representing ~13% of global GDP, expanding market access for UK exporters (e.g., services, agriculture, manufacturing), increasing competition to lower consumer prices, and boosting prosperity via comparative advantage and voluntary exchange—core to wealth creation through liberty and private property. Deleting this Act would forfeit reciprocal trade liberalization benefits, leaving UK firms disadvantaged against competitors in CPTPP markets, distorting incentives, raising costs, and reducing overall wealth; no unilateral action substitutes for this institutional framework of mutual concessions that prevents beggar-thy-neighbor policies.

items.title: Pedicabs (London) Act 2024
items.summary: The Pedicabs (London) Act 2024 empowers Transport for London to regulate pedicabs (rickshaws) in Greater London, including licensing of vehicles, drivers and operators, fare controls, safety standards, operational restrictions, and enforcement mechanisms like fines and seizures.
items.reason: This act imposes licensing fees, compliance burdens (insurance, testing, standards), fare controls, and potential supply restrictions, creating barriers to entry that reduce competition, raise fares, limit supply of affordable transport, and favor incumbents while harming low-capital entrepreneurs; enforcement bureaucracy distorts incentives, with unseen costs including lost jobs, higher consumer prices, and stifled innovation—markets self-regulate via reputation and competition, handling safety/nuisances better without state intervention.

items.title: Safety of Rwanda (Asylum and Immigration) Act 2024 (repealed)
items.summary: The Safety of Rwanda (Asylum and Immigration) Act 2024 declares Rwanda a safe country for removals, mandates decision-makers (including courts) to treat it as such, disapplies parts of the Human Rights Act 1998, limits challenges except on individual circumstances, and empowers ministers to decide on ECtHR interim measures, aiming to deter illegal migration via deportations to Rwanda.
items.reason: This Act exemplifies government intervention with high fiscal costs (billions spent on a stalled scheme yielding few deportations) and unintended consequences like eroding judicial independence by dictating court outcomes, distorting immigration incentives without reducing crossings, diverting taxpayer funds from productive uses, and creating dependency on foreign deals rather than robust border institutions; deleting it would eliminate waste, restore rule of law, and allow market-driven solutions like streamlined legal migration to enhance prosperity without overriding liberties.

items.title: Investigatory Powers (Amendment) Act 2024
items.summary: The Investigatory Powers (Amendment) Act 2024 amends the Investigatory Powers Act 2016 to expand intelligence services' access to bulk personal datasets (BPDs) with low/no privacy expectation via new individual/category authorisations (with Judicial Commissioner approval), introduces warrants for third-party BPDs, extends warrant durations, adds oversight (deputy/temporary commissioners), adjusts communications data rules, retention/technical notices, safeguards for MPs/journalists, and restores some disclosure powers.
items.reason: This legislation vastly expands state surveillance powers over private data, imposing retention and capability mandates on telecoms/postal operators that raise compliance costs, distort incentives for innovation, and risk business flight (e.g., non-UK operators now included). Bulk access to 'low privacy' public data enables mission creep, eroding privacy as a cornerstone of liberty and property rights; safeguards/oversight are bureaucratic layers unlikely to prevent abuse given government incentives for overreach. Unseen costs include chilled data sharing/free speech, reduced tech investment/prosperity, and institutional entrenchment of central surveillance planning over spontaneous market security solutions like targeted warrants. Politicians' security rhetoric ignores these tradeoffs, harming individualism and UK's economic competitiveness.

items.title: The Online Safety (List of Overseas Regulators) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Regulations specifying overseas regulators with whom OFCOM may cooperate under section 114 of the Online Safety Act 2023, for facilitating online regulatory functions or related criminal investigations.
items.reason: This enables bureaucratic international cooperation on online safety regulation, amplifying the Online Safety Act's interventionist framework. Unseen costs include administrative burdens, potential mission creep toward global regulatory harmonization, erosion of UK sovereignty, and distortion of incentives for private platforms to innovate freely; harms like cross-border censorship overreach outweigh minimal benefits, as cooperation could occur via voluntary agreements or existing treaties without statutory lists.

items.title: The Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023 (Commencement No. 5 and Transitional Provision) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Commencement regulations activating sections 237 and 238 of the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023, which amend the Childcare Act 2006 to allow childminding on non-domestic premises (removing domestic premises requirement) and increase maximum childminders/assistants from 3 to 4 total, deregulating childcare provision.
items.reason: Deleting these regulations would prevent deregulatory changes, maintaining stricter limits on premises and group sizes that restrict childcare supply, raise costs for parents, limit workforce participation (especially for mothers), and distort incentives for providers; without this, market entry barriers persist, unseen costs include reduced economic liberty and prosperity from childcare shortages, whereas these changes promote supply growth and choice without evident failure in achieving safer, more accessible childcare via private provision.

items.title: The Official Controls (Extension of Transitional Periods) and Plant Health (Frequency of Checks) (Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amends EU Official Controls Regulation (2017/625) and related UK instruments to extend the transitional staging period (TSP) for eased official controls and reduced frequency of checks on certain fruit, vegetables, plants, plant products, and other objects imported into Great Britain from EU Member States, Liechtenstein, or Switzerland, through relevant ports (now including Swansea), until 1 July 2025.
items.reason: This legislation temporarily reduces regulatory burdens on post-Brexit plant imports, minimizing border delays, costs, and supply distortions that would raise food prices and harm consumers and businesses. Deleting it would end the TSP earlier, imposing stricter checks sooner, creating unintended shortages, higher costs, and reduced trade without proven risk reduction justifying the economic harm, as no significant impacts are foreseen.

items.title: The Air Navigation (Restriction of Flying) (Alloa, Clackmannanshire) (Emergency) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Emergency regulations imposing temporary restrictions on flying over Alloa, Clackmannanshire.
items.reason: This ad-hoc, location-specific flying restriction infringes on individual liberty and property rights of aircraft owners and pilots, distorting incentives for air travel and imposing unseen economic costs such as rerouting flights, increased fuel use, and reduced efficiency in a vital transport sector. Emergency powers like this create moral hazard, encouraging government overreach without market-driven safety mechanisms or private liability insurance, which could handle localized risks more dynamically without blanket prohibitions. Deleting it removes a barrier to free movement and innovation in aviation.

items.title: The School Teachers’ Incentive Payments (England) (Amendment) Order 2024
items.summary: Amendment to the School Teachers’ Incentive Payments (England) Order 2019, renaming the 'Levelling Up Premium' scheme to 'Targeted Retention Incentive' in a list of payments exempt from being treated as remuneration under section 122(1) of the Education Act 2002.
items.reason: This minor administrative name change perpetuates a government scheme distorting public sector teacher pay by exempting incentives from remuneration calculations, hiding true costs in pensions and budgets. Unseen consequences include incentivizing endless bureaucratic tinkering with pay structures, inflating public spending without market discipline, crowding out private education alternatives, and reinforcing state monopoly control over labor markets, reducing incentives for efficiency and innovation in schooling.

items.title: The Customs (Tariff and Miscellaneous Amendments) (No. 3) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Technical amendments to UK customs tariff regulations, including reducing duty on dibromomethane, updating commodity codes, quota pro-rating methodologies, preferential trade arrangement documents for various FTAs, suspensions of import duties, and CPTPP alignment; no significant policy changes.
items.reason: While tariffs inherently distort markets and reduce prosperity by raising costs and limiting choice, these amendments include a duty reduction and facilitate FTAs (e.g., CPTPP) by aligning documents, correcting errors, and updating quotas/codes. Deleting would cause administrative chaos—outdated codes leading to compliance errors, incorrect duties applied, unupdated FTAs hindering preferential access, and higher effective costs for importers—making British businesses and consumers worse off without eliminating underlying tariffs.

items.title: The Financial Services Act 2012 (Relevant Functions in relation to Complaints Scheme) (Amendment) Order 2024
items.summary: This Order amends the Financial Services Act 2012 (Relevant Functions in relation to Complaints Scheme) Order 2014 to exclude the FCA's rulemaking powers under regulations 22 and 34 of the Securitisation Regulations 2024 from the statutory complaints scheme, following prior amendments.
items.reason: This narrow, technical amendment adds pointless bureaucratic fine-tuning to an already bloated regulatory complaints scheme for the FCA, perpetuating the regulatory state's complexity without delivering tangible benefits to liberty or prosperity. The official impact assessment notes no significant effects, underscoring its superfluity. Unseen costs include ongoing administrative maintenance, distorted incentives for regulators to avoid scrutiny on other functions, and statute book clutter that hinders institutional simplification. Deleting it streamlines oversight without harming citizens, as the complaints scheme itself is a low-value government layer that distorts market signals rather than protecting consumers effectively.

items.title: The Council Tax (Prescribed Classes of Dwellings and Consequential Amendments) (England) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amendments to the Council Tax (Prescribed Classes of Dwellings) (England) Regulations 2003, prescribing additional classes of dwellings exempt from empty homes premiums (e.g., those marketed for sale/let up to 12 months, undergoing major works or probate, armed forces related) and second homes premiums (e.g., job-related, caravans/boats, planning restrictions, annexes).
items.reason: These amendments limit the scope of punitive council tax premiums on empty and second homes by carving out exceptions for legitimate circumstances like marketing properties, renovations, probate, or specific uses. Deleting them would expand authorities' power to impose extra taxes, raising costs for property owners, distorting housing markets, discouraging investment and sales/renovations, and harming incentives for voluntary market solutions—British citizens, especially property owners and those in transition, would be worse off with broader government intervention that fails to account for real-world complexities and unintended reductions in supply.

items.title: The Seafarers' Wages Act 2023 (Commencement) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Regulations bringing into force the remaining provisions of the Seafarers’ Wages Act 2023, which requires ship operators on domestic UK voyages to ensure seafarers receive at least the national minimum wage and submit wage reports.
items.reason: Mandates minimum wages and bureaucratic reporting, distorting shipping labor markets by raising costs, risking job losses for low-skilled seafarers, reducing UK fleet competitiveness, and creating compliance burdens that increase prices for goods transport without evidence of net benefits; free markets would better match wages to productivity and supply/demand.

items.title: The Corporation Tax (Certification as Low-Budget Film) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Regulations defining budget and creative connection conditions for additional corporation tax relief on low-budget British films, including certification requirements.
items.reason: This tax subsidy distorts market incentives in the film industry, channeling resources into government-favored low-budget productions rather than consumer-driven demand, fostering dependency on state support, enabling rent-seeking by producers, and imposing unseen administrative costs and compliance burdens that reduce overall economic efficiency without evidence of market failure.

items.title: The Pension Protection Fund and Occupational Pension Schemes (Levy Ceiling) Order 2024 (revoked)
items.summary: Statutory instrument setting the Pension Protection Fund levy ceiling at £1,349,215,811 for the financial year from 1 April 2024, increased by 8.2% in line with earnings growth, capping mandatory levies on occupational pension schemes to fund PPF compensation for failed schemes.
items.reason: Mandating levies on private pension schemes to fund a government-run insurance pool socialises risk, creates moral hazard (encouraging riskier schemes knowing of backstop), diverts capital from productive uses, raises costs for employers and workers reducing pension supply and distorting incentives, while administrative burdens and unintended consequences like reduced defined-benefit offerings harm prosperity; market-based private insurance would better align incentives without coercion.

items.title: The Finance (No. 2) Act 2024 (Applications for Certification as Low-Budget Film: Appointed Day) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Appoints 30th October 2024 as the date from which applications for low-budget film certificates can be made, enabling additional corporation tax relief for qualifying UK-connected low-budget films under the Finance (No. 2) Act 2024 amendments to the Corporation Tax Act 2009.
items.reason: This regulation enables targeted tax relief (a subsidy) for low-budget films, distorting market incentives by favoring one industry over others, encouraging rent-seeking and lobbying, and diverting resources from more productive uses. Such sector-specific interventions create unseen costs like reduced tax neutrality, potential for cronyism, and dependency on government support, undermining liberty and efficient private allocation of capital. Deleting it prevents activation of this distortionary policy, allowing markets to fund films based on genuine demand rather than fiscal favoritism.

items.title: The Parole Board (Amendment) Rules 2024
items.summary: Amendments to the Parole Board Rules 2019 via a Statutory Instrument, introducing procedural efficiencies such as delegation of non-judicial functions to staff (with review), allowing hearings without prisoner presence if in interests of justice, updates for IPP/DPP regime changes, and minor administrative clarifications like standardized timelines and error correction limits.
items.reason: These are minor procedural tweaks to an essential state institution for managing parole, enhancing efficiency without imposing costs on private citizens or distorting incentives. Deleting would revert to less efficient rules, delaying decisions, raising taxpayer costs for Parole Board operations, risking procedural unfairness or public safety gaps (e.g., delayed revocations), and undermining the rule of law that protects liberty and property. No unseen consequences like market distortions; achieves fairer, faster justice spontaneously through better incentives for staff and panels.

items.title: The Pensions (Abolition of Lifetime Allowance Charge etc) (No. 2) Regulations 2024
items.summary: These Regulations make technical consequential amendments to primary and secondary legislation following the abolition of the pension lifetime allowance (LTA) and its associated charge by Finance Acts 2023 and 2024. They update definitions, penalties, lump sum allowances, death benefits, transitional protections (e.g., primary, enhanced, fixed protections), information requirements, and tax treatments for pension schemes, ensuring coherent application from tax year 2024-25.
items.reason: Abolishing the LTA reduces government distortion of private pension savings and incentives to save, aligning with liberty and private property principles. These amendments are essential housekeeping to prevent legal inconsistencies, calculation errors, or disputes in pension taxation post-abolition; without them, pensioners and schemes face uncertainty, potential over-taxation, or withheld benefits under outdated rules, harming savers and prosperity. They achieve smooth transition to LTA-free regime without which desired tax relief would falter due to incoherent institutions.

items.title: The Payment Services (Amendment) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amendments to the Payment Services Regulations 2017 allowing payment service providers (PSPs) to delay execution of payment orders if they suspect fraud or dishonesty by a third party (e.g., payee), subject to time limits, payer notification, and liability rules for resulting charges/interest.
items.reason: British citizens would be worse off without this legislation as PSPs would revert to strict execution timelines, enabling more authorised push payment (APP) fraud where victims' funds are transferred to scammers before reversal, resulting in billions in direct property losses, eroded trust in payment systems, higher reimbursement costs passed to consumers, and reduced incentives for digital commerce. This achieves fraud protection—a core property rights safeguard—by permitting prudent delays that market competition alone has not sufficiently enabled under prior rigid rules.

items.title: The Associated British Ports (Immingham Eastern Ro-Ro Terminal) Development Consent Order 2024
items.summary: Development Consent Order under the Planning Act 2008 authorising Associated British Ports to construct, operate, and maintain a new Roll-on/Roll-off (RoRo) terminal with three berths, additional marine infrastructure, dredged berthing pocket, and associated development at the Port of Immingham, including powers of compulsory land acquisition.
items.reason: This Order grants special state-backed privileges to a private company, including compulsory purchase of private property, violating core principles of private property rights and voluntary exchange. While it may enable port expansion for trade, markets would allocate resources efficiently through negotiation; holdout problems do not justify eminent domain, which distorts incentives, encourages cronyism, fosters rent-seeking for similar Orders, raises unseen costs like displaced locals' welfare, environmental harms, and reduced competition among port developers. Deleting it promotes liberty, forcing private solutions or alternative sites, preventing government picking winners and perpetuating interventionist institutions that hinder long-term prosperity.

items.title: The Seafarers’ Wages Regulations 2024
items.summary: Regulations implementing the Seafarers’ Wages Act 2023, requiring harbour authorities to obtain national minimum wage (NMW) equivalence declarations from operators of ships entering UK harbours 120+ times yearly, imposing surcharges for non-compliance or inconsistent operation, and enabling refusal of harbour access for unpaid surcharges; includes detailed calculations for remuneration, hours, and rates.
items.reason: Imposes bureaucratic minimum wage equivalence on international seafarers, distorting global labor markets and shipping incentives; raises operator costs via compliance, surcharges, and access risks, leading to unseen consequences like route avoidance, reduced UK port traffic and trade, higher goods prices for consumers, job losses in logistics/harbors, and administrative burdens on authorities—without evidence of net prosperity gains, as free markets better match wages to productivity.

items.title: The Family Procedure (Amendment) Rules 2024
items.summary: Amendments to Family Procedure Rules 2010: inserts rule 6.23A for special service provisions on persons reasonably believed to be in refuges (protecting likely domestic abuse victims), with consequential amendments; adds 'Nominated Circuit Judge' to those who can refuse permission for meritless appeals without a hearing.
items.reason: Deleting this would leave vulnerable refuge residents (often domestic abuse victims) exposed to easier location and service by abusers, undermining their liberty and safety—outcomes not spontaneously achieved without targeted procedural protection. The appeal streamlining reduces court delays and costs, enhancing efficiency in dispute resolution without distorting incentives or markets. Unintended costs are minimal as it's narrow, procedural, and liberty-preserving rather than regulatory overreach.

items.title: The Architects (Fees, Electronic Communications and Miscellaneous Amendments) (Amendment) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amendments to the Architects (Fees, Electronic Communications and Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations 2022, specifying liability for fees charged by the Architects Registration Board, payment methods, and requiring publication of fee information.
items.reason: This minor administrative regulation imposes unnecessary bureaucratic prescriptions on fee collection and publication for the Architects Registration Board, entailing compliance costs, reduced administrative flexibility, and perpetuation of an occupational licensing regime that creates barriers to entry, stifles competition in architecture services, inflates costs for consumers, and diverts resources from productive uses—unseen consequences outweigh any trivial clarity provided, as private entities manage fees efficiently without such mandates.

items.title: The Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act 2024 (Commencement No. 1) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Regulations activating provisions of the Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act 2024 that amend the Building Safety Act 2022 to clarify protections for leaseholders regarding building safety responsibilities.
items.reason: This legislation entrenches further government intervention into private property contracts and the leasehold market, distorting incentives by shifting safety remediation costs and liabilities away from market-negotiated agreements toward state-mandated protections. Unintended consequences include higher compliance costs for developers and freeholders, reduced supply of new housing (especially apartments), elevated rents and property prices harming affordability, and perpetuation of the UK's inefficient leasehold system rather than allowing market-driven reforms via reputation, insurance, and voluntary contracts. Private property rights and tort law suffice for safety without bureaucratic overrides that hinder prosperity.

items.title: The Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023 (Miscellaneous Amendment) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amendments to childcare regulations under the Childcare Act 2006, implementing changes from the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023 to allow childminding wholly on non-domestic premises, increase maximum group sizes for childminders (from 3 to 4 total people), adjust registration thresholds, and make consequential updates to related instruments on registration, fees, exemptions, and welfare requirements.
items.reason: These deregulatory amendments expand the supply of childcare by removing restrictions on premises and group sizes, reducing barriers to entry for providers, increasing competition, and lowering costs for parents without new mandates or taxes. Deleting them would revert to stricter rules, distorting incentives, limiting options for working parents, reducing labour force participation (especially for mothers), and harming economic liberty and prosperity; market incentives would not spontaneously recreate this flexibility amid existing regulatory burdens.

items.title: The Securitisation Regulations 2024
items.summary: Designates securitisation activities (originator, sponsor, lender, SPV roles, and selling positions to UK retail clients) as regulated 'designated activities' under FSMA 2000, empowers FCA/PRA to make rules/directions, restates/modifies EU Securitisation Regulation provisions on registration, STS labelling/notifications, due diligence for investors, enforcement, with de minimis impact claimed.
items.reason: This adds layers of regulatory oversight, compliance costs, and bureaucratic rule-making to securitisation markets, distorting incentives for credit creation and funding (e.g., higher costs for pooling loans into securities reduces supply/affordability of mortgages/auto loans). Unseen consequences include stifled innovation, competitive disadvantage for UK finance post-Brexit, and moral hazard from regulator reliance over market discipline/reputation; crises stem from monetary policy/moral hazard, not lack of such rules, harming prosperity/liberty without proven net benefits.

items.title: The M3 Junction 9 Development Consent (Correction) Order 2024
items.summary: Correction order fixing correctable errors in the M3 Junction 9 Development Consent Order 2024 (S.I. 2024/752), a development consent for infrastructure improvements under the Planning Act 2008.
items.reason: Deletion would leave errors uncorrected in the underlying development consent order, risking legal challenges, project delays, or invalidation, perpetuating congestion at a key motorway junction. This hinders commerce, safety, and economic productivity—outcomes unlikely to self-correct without formal legal fixes. Though government-led infrastructure distorts markets via taxation, this minor correction enables a project that demonstrably improves transport efficiency with minimal ongoing regulatory burden.

items.title: The Investigatory Powers (Amendment) Act 2024 (Commencement No. 1 and Transitional Provisions) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Commencement regulations bringing into force most provisions of the Investigatory Powers (Amendment) Act 2024, amending the 2016 Act to expand surveillance powers on bulk personal datasets, communications data retention, third-party data access, oversight, and safeguards for legislators and journalists.
items.reason: This activates broad state surveillance over private communications and personal data held by telecom operators, infringing on privacy as a core individual liberty and property right. Compliance burdens distort telecom markets by raising costs, reducing competition, and deterring innovation in secure technologies. Unseen consequences include chilled free speech, eroded public trust in digital services, potential for mission creep and abuse by agencies, and illusory security gains outweighed by systemic risks to liberty; narrower, judicially targeted powers could suffice without mass data regimes.

items.title: The Individual Savings Account (Amendment) (No. 2) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amendments to the Individual Savings Account Regulations 1998 to enable fractional interests in stocks and shares ISAs, adjust declarations for individuals without national insurance numbers, specify proportional pricing for fractional transactions, qualify certain shares/investments, and simplify partial transfer information requirements.
items.reason: These amendments expand investment flexibility in tax-advantaged ISAs by permitting fractional shares, reducing entry barriers for smaller investors, promoting broader capital participation, and enhancing savings incentives without distorting markets or imposing new burdens. Deleting them would limit options, reduce individual liberty in wealth-building, hinder capital formation, and leave savers worse off under prior restrictive rules, as the institution of ISAs demonstrably encourages voluntary savings over taxation that discourages it.

items.title: The Child Trust Funds (Amendment) (No. 2) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amendments to the Child Trust Funds Regulations 2004 to allow fractional interests in shares of investment trusts and listed companies as qualifying investments in child trust fund accounts, including rules on aggregation, pricing, and stakeholder account status.
items.reason: Deleting this would restrict investment options in existing Child Trust Funds, limiting children's savings to fewer, potentially lower-return assets by excluding fractional shares in equities. This legislation enables broader market exposure and diversification, promoting long-term wealth growth through private property investments, which would not occur without these specific regulatory permissions in the government-backed scheme. British citizens with CTFs would be worse off with reduced growth potential and less liberty in asset choice.

items.title: The Building Societies Act 1986 (Amendment of Small Business Turnover Limit) Order 2024
items.summary: Amends the Building Societies Act 1986 by increasing the turnover limit for small businesses from £1,000,000 to £6,500,000, allowing up to 10% of their deposits to be disregarded in calculating a building society's total funds (to maintain at least 50% from individual shares).
items.reason: Deleting this legislation would revert to the stricter £1m threshold, limiting building societies' flexibility to accept deposits from a narrower range of small/medium businesses. This would constrain funding sources, potentially reducing mortgage supply, increasing borrowing costs, and harming savers through less competition—making citizens worse off. The adjustment adapts the mutuality rule to current economic scale (e.g., inflation), achieving its goal of balanced funding without an outdated limit forcing unnecessary restrictions or conversions to banks.

items.title: The Air Navigation (Restriction of Flying) (Alloa, Clackmannanshire) (Emergency) (Revocation) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Revokes the emergency Air Navigation (Restriction of Flying) (Alloa, Clackmannanshire) Regulations, lifting a temporary no-fly zone over a specific area.
items.reason: British citizens, particularly aviators and residents near Alloa, would be worse off if deleted, as it could reinstate an unnecessary flying restriction with no ongoing emergency justification, curtailing individual liberty in air navigation without achieving any protective outcome; this revocation efficiently restores pre-emergency freedoms via clear institutional mechanism, preventing regulatory persistence.

items.title: The Budget Responsibility Act 2024 (Commencement) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Regulations commencing section 1 of the Budget Responsibility Act 2024, requiring the Treasury or OBR to assess fiscally significant ministerial announcements with independent reports under the 2011 Act.
items.reason: Deleting this would remove mandatory independent scrutiny of major fiscal announcements, allowing politicians to make unchecked promises of spending or tax changes that often lead to deficits, higher future taxes, inflation, and crowding out of private investment—outcomes British citizens would suffer without this institutional check, as evidenced by historical fiscal irresponsibility; the OBR's role enforces transparency and discipline that politicians lack incentives to self-impose.

items.title: The Sea Fisheries (Amendment) (No. 2) Regulations 2024
items.summary: Amendments to assimilated EU sea fisheries law: introduces minimum conservation sizes for lemon sole, turbot, brill, and crawfish; minimum mesh size and engine power limits for flyseining gear in specified English waters; removes seabass catch limits from EU regulation to be managed via vessel licences.
